structured_choices.data_source: Structured EHR dataset: 10,000 patients with chronic diseases over 5 years. Data includes demographics, diagnostic codes (ICD-9/ICD-10), procedure codes, medication records, lab values, vital signs, and admission/discharge timestamps. Source: single health system EHR database. Time period: 5 years of historical records; each patient has a defined index discharge event with a 30-day readmission window (standard clinical definition) and 180-day look-back period for feature construction.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary readmission outcome: 1 = unplanned hospital readmission within 30 days of discharge; 0 = no readmission within 30 days. Readmission is defined as any inpatient admission (excluding scheduled/elective procedures, documented in admission type field). Only index discharges are included; patients with multiple discharges contribute one observation each (the first qualifying discharge during the study period).
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split by discharge date, with stratification by readmission class within each time window. Training: discharges from year 1–3 (approximately 6,000 patients). Validation: discharges from year 4, first half (approximately 1,500 patients). Test: discharges from year 4, second half and year 5 (approximately 2,500 patients). Justification: Temporal split prevents information leakage—the model trains only on historical data and is validated/tested on future discharge cohorts, respecting the causal order of the prediction task. Stratified sampling within each window ensures class balance representation across splits, critical given anticipated class imbalance (typically 15–25% readmission rate in chronic disease populations). This reflects deployment: a trained model is applied to prospective discharge cohorts.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (imputation, scaling, encoding) are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets. Specifically: (1) mean/median imputation for missing lab values and vitals is computed on training set; (2) standardization (z-score scaling) for continuous features fit on training set; (3) one-hot encoding for categorical variables (diagnostic codes, admission source) fit on training set vocabulary. This prevents data leakage and ensures test-set performance is an honest estimate of deployment performance on unseen patient cohorts.
structured_choices.features: Temporal feature set constructed from 180-day look-back window prior to index discharge: (1) Demographics: age, sex, race/ethnicity (if available without bias concerns), insurance type; (2) Clinical history: number of prior admissions in past 12 months, number of distinct diagnoses, Charlson comorbidity index, count of emergency department visits in past 6 months; (3) Index admission features: admission source (ED vs. scheduled), length of stay, primary and secondary diagnoses (top 50 ICD codes via frequency), procedure counts; (4) Medication burden: number of distinct medications at discharge, presence of high-risk medication classes (anticoagulants, opioids, insulin); (5) Recent lab/vital abnormalities: presence of abnormal lab results in final 48 hours of admission (flagged by clinical reference ranges), vital sign instability (pulse, BP variability). Temporal encoding: a) count of admission events in rolling 30/60/90-day windows (captures temporal density); b) time since last admission (continuous); c) time elapsed between prior admissions (coefficient of variation, captures visit regularity). No features encode the label directly; no future information (post-discharge events) is included.
structured_choices.model: Hybrid CNN-LSTM model: (1) CNN component (1D convolutions): processes the sequence of temporal features (rolling window statistics) to extract local temporal patterns and regularities in visit behavior; (2) LSTM component: operates on the full sequence of admission/lab events to capture long-range dependencies and gradual deterioration trends. Architecture: CNN layers (32 and 64 filters, kernel size 3) → max pooling → LSTM (128 units, return_sequences=False) → fully connected layers (64 units, ReLU) → output (sigmoid for binary classification). Dropout (0.3) and L2 regularization (0.001) applied to prevent overfitting. Sequence length: padded/truncated to 100 timesteps (corresponding to ~6-month visit history at typical visit frequencies). Input normalization performed post-fit-on-train-only scaling.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression trained on the same feature set (but without explicit temporal encoding—uses aggregated static/summary features). The baseline receives identical preprocessing, hyperparameter tuning effort (via validation set grid search over regularization and class weights), and is evaluated on the identical test set. Justification: Logistic regression is the standard structured-data model in clinical prediction and represents the current clinical standard for readmission prediction. Providing equivalent tuning effort ensures the comparison isolates the value of the CNN-LSTM temporal architecture, not tuning intensity. The same feature set (rather than hand-crafted features) ensures feature engineering complexity is held constant.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC). Justification: Readmission prediction is a ranking task—clinicians and care coordinators use model scores to stratify patients into intervention tiers (high-risk receive intensive follow-up, low-risk receive standard care). AUROC measures the model's ability to discriminate across the full ranking, which directly translates to the operational goal: correctly identifying high-risk patients. AUROC is also robust to class imbalance (does not weight FP and FN equally) and is threshold-agnostic, avoiding arbitrary cutoff selection. Secondary evaluation at a clinically relevant operating point (e.g., 80% sensitivity, used in some health systems for intensive intervention eligibility) is conducted to assess practical threshold performance.
secondary_metrics: Precision-Recall AUC (AUPRC): better aligned with class imbalance; directly reflects positive predictive value at varying recall levels, relevant for intervention resource allocation.
Sensitivity at 90% specificity: ensures the model does not miss a high proportion of true readmission cases (clinical safety requirement).

Calibration (Expected Calibration Error, ECE): readmission risk scores are communicated to clinicians and used for resource planning; miscalibrated scores lead to incorrect intervention intensity. ECE measures systematic over/under-confidence.
Subgroup performance (by age group, comorbidity level, admission source): validates that model improvements generalize across patient heterogeneity and detects potential disparities.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (year 4, first half, ~1,500 patients) is used for hyperparameter tuning (CNN-LSTM: learning rate, dropout, regularization; Logistic regression: regularization strength and class weights). A nested cross-validation is not performed; instead, a single validation hold-out is used because temporal structure is already respected in the train-val-test split (temporal CV would introduce leakage). The model (CNN-LSTM and baseline) achieving the best validation AUROC is then evaluated once on the test set.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (year 4, second half and year 5, ~2,500 patients) is held until final evaluation. No hyperparameter tuning, model selection, or threshold tuning is performed on the test set. The final trained models (CNN-LSTM and logistic regression) are applied to the test set in a single forward pass; all metrics (AUROC, AUPRC, sensitivity, calibration, subgroup performance) are computed on the test set and reported with 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrap resampling, stratified by class).
structured_choices.confound_controls: Confound: Variability in patient follow-up care (some patients receive intensive outpatient follow-up, others do not), which affects readmission rates independent of model predictions. Control strategies: (1) Feature inclusion: proxy variables for follow-up intensity are included in the feature set—number of outpatient visits in 30 days pre-discharge (when available), pharmacy refill frequency, care coordination flags. The model can learn associations between follow-up intensity and readmission, but predictions reflect observed follow-up patterns, not ideal follow-up. Interpretation caveat: the model predicts readmission given current follow-up practices, not counterfactual readmission under different follow-up regimens. (2) Subgroup analysis: readmission rates and model performance are stratified by proxy follow-up intensity (high vs. low visit frequency in 30 days pre-discharge). If model performance degrades significantly in low-follow-up groups, this signals uncontrolled confounding and limits generalization. (3) Explicit documentation: any known differences in follow-up care by health system site (if multi-site data), insurance status, or geography are flagged; the experiment remains single-site/cohort unless the design explicitly addresses site-level variation via stratification. Scope limitation: This design does not establish causality (i.e., that the model's predictions cause changes in readmission). Rather, it establishes predictive accuracy—the model's ability to discriminate future readmission outcomes given observed covariates and follow-up care patterns.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Hybrid CNN-LSTM vs. Logistic Regression for Hospital Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data and Cohort Definition

The analysis uses structured EHR data from 10,000 chronic disease patients spanning 5 years. Each observation represents a single index discharge event with a 30-day follow-up window and a 180-day look-back period for feature construction. The binary readmission label is defined as any unplanned hospital readmission within 30 days of discharge (standard clinical definition). Elective and scheduled procedures are excluded from the readmission definition.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

To respect causal ordering and prevent information leakage, the data is divided by discharge date into three sequential windows: (1) Training: discharges from years 1–3 (~6,000 patients); (2) Validation: discharges from year 4, first half (~1,500 patients); (3) Test: discharges from year 4, second half and year 5 (~2,500 patients). Within each window, the cohort is stratified by readmission class (0/1) to maintain class balance representation. This temporal split reflects deployment reality—a model trained on historical discharges is applied to prospective cohorts. The stratified component ensures that class imbalance (anticipated at 15–25% readmission rate) does not bias any split.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Features are constructed from the 180-day look-back window prior to discharge and encode four dimensions: (1) Demographics and baseline risk (age, comorbidity index); (2) Temporal utilization patterns (prior admission counts in rolling 30/60/90-day windows, time since last admission, admission frequency stability); (3) Index admission characteristics (length of stay, admission source, primary diagnosis); (4) Medication and clinical instability (medication burden, recent abnormal labs, vital sign variability). Temporal features explicitly capture sequence structure and irregular visit intervals, enabling the LSTM to learn patterns of clinical deterioration.

All preprocessing is fit on the training set only: mean imputation for missing values, z-score standardization for continuous features, and one-hot encoding for categorical variables (fitted on training vocabulary). These transformations are applied identically to validation and test sets, preventing leakage and ensuring honest out-of-sample performance estimates.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The hybrid CNN-LSTM model processes temporal sequences: a 1D CNN (32–64 filters) extracts local patterns in visit dynamics, followed by LSTM layers (128 units) that capture long-range dependencies. The output passes through fully connected layers (64 units, ReLU) to a sigmoid output. Dropout (0.3) and L2 regularization (0.001) prevent overfitting.

The baseline is logistic regression trained on the identical feature set and preprocessed using the same fit-on-train approach. Both models undergo equivalent hyperparameter tuning on the validation set (learning rate, regularization strength, class weights). This ensures the comparison isolates the value of the temporal architecture, not differential tuning effort.

### 5. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set using AUROC as the selection criterion. The optimal models (CNN-LSTM and logistic regression) are then frozen and evaluated once on the test set. No model selection, threshold tuning, or metric inspection occurs on the test set.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC). This metric reflects the operational goal—correctly ranking patients by readmission risk across the full spectrum to guide stratified intervention. AUROC is robust to class imbalance and avoids arbitrary threshold selection.

**Secondary metrics:** Precision-Recall AUC (more sensitive to class imbalance than AUROC), sensitivity at 90% specificity (ensures clinically safe false negative rates), and calibration (Expected Calibration Error—essential because readmission scores are communicated to clinicians). Subgroup analysis by age, comorbidity level, and admission source assesses generalization and detects disparities.

### 7. Addressing Confounding

Variable follow-up care intensity is addressed through feature inclusion (outpatient visit frequency, pharmacy refill patterns, care coordination flags) and subgroup stratification. The model learns associations between follow-up intensity and readmission but makes predictions conditional on observed care patterns—not counterfactual predictions. Subgroup performance is tracked; substantial degradation in low-follow-up groups signals uncontrolled confounding. Results are scoped as predictive accuracy given current care practices, not causal estimates of readmission determinants.

### 8. Success Criteria

The hypothesis is supported if the CNN-LSTM achieves meaningfully higher validation and test AUROC than logistic regression (e.g., ≥0.03 absolute improvement), with comparable or superior calibration and sensitivity, and consistent subgroup performance across follow-up intensity strata.
